title: Chocolate on Wool
stainChemistry: Wool is a protein fiber (keratin) with overlapping cuticle scales exquisitely sensitive to pH and mechanical stress. Chocolate on wool is a three-component stain: cocoa butter (lipid, wicks into medullary layer), milk casein (protein, bonds to keratin through sulfide bridges), tannins (pigments, embed in fiber matrix). Wool's cuticle scales lock together (felting) under heat, alkalinity, or friction—permanent structural damage. Protein enzymes digest wool's keratin protein along with chocolate's milk protein, destroying fiber strength. The challenge: remove chocolate's three components while keeping water below 30°C, pH 4.5-7, and zero friction.
whyThisWorks: Cold water + pH-neutral soap mobilizes cocoa butter without raising pH (alkalinity triggers felting). Avoiding enzymes entirely protects wool's keratin structure. Mild acetic acid (vinegar) gently mobilizes tannin residue without dropping pH below safety floor (4.5). Optional: dilute H₂O₂ on white wool only for yellowing (cocoa butter oxidation). This protocol removes stain components while preventing felting.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush the stain with COOL water (cold, never tepid or warm). Work from back of fabric. Do NOT wring, twist, or agitate—fiber friction causes felting. Blot only, pressing gently with cloth. This removes loose chocolate particles and some cocoa butter before detergent step.

spottingProtocol.agent: Wool-safe detergent at neutral pH (NSD, Dreft, lanolin-based) at 1 tsp per 2 cups cold water
spottingProtocol.equipment: Basin, soft cloth, pH paper optional
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix detergent in cold water to neutral pH (test with pH paper if available—target 6.5-7.0). Immerse stained area gently. Wool fibers slide past each other under lubricating action of mild soap, mobilizing cocoa butter without heat. Keep hands still, water temperature constant. DO NOT agitate or rub. After 5-10 minutes, rinse immediately with cold water. Longer soaking risks fiber stress.

Vinegar at pH 4.0-4.5 is SAFE for wool and mobilizes chocolate tannins (polyphenol pigments) remaining in fiber. Gently blot or rinse with dilute vinegar solution. This step also neutralizes any alkaline detergent residue—important to prevent felting. DO NOT leave vinegar on wool—rinse immediately with cool water to stop acid action.

spottingProtocol.agent: Optional: Dilute H₂O₂ for yellowing on WHITE WOOL ONLY
spottingProtocol.technique: Cautious soak, test first on hidden area
spottingProtocol.equipment: Hidden seam test area, 3% H₂O₂ solution
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: TEST 1 minute on hidden seam first. If safe: 3-5 minutes on stain maximum
spottingProtocol.instruction: For white wool only—cocoa butter oxidizes yellow/brown over time. If yellowing persists after vinegar step, test dilute H₂O₂ on hidden seam for 1 minute. Check for color change or weakening. If safe, apply to stain for maximum 5 minutes (longer risks yellowing). DO NOT use on colored wool. Blot, rinse thoroughly with cool water. This oxidative treatment is optional and only for white wool.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cool water with final trace of vinegar per gallon
spottingProtocol.instruction: A final very weak vinegar rinse helps set wool fiber cuticles in smooth position and prevents felting. This micro-acidification is a professional technique. Blot gently and press out excess water with your hands—do NOT wring.

homeSolutions.agent: Three-part stain on a protein fiber — work in order
homeSolutions.instruction: Chocolate on wool is the hardest home stain combination in the library: cocoa butter (fat) + milk casein (protein) + cocoa tannin (pigment) on a fiber that CANNOT tolerate enzymes, heat, or alkaline. You have fewer tools than on cotton. Two realistic outcomes: caught fresh with cold-and-gentle chemistry, most comes out. Set or colored wool, you're looking at professional solvent spotting. Start with low expectations and escalate early if needed.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden seam
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab a drop of wool-safe detergent (Eucalan, Woolite, Soak) mixed with cold water on an inside seam. Wait 60 seconds, blot with a white cloth. If the seam loses color or feels rougher, stop — go to step 7. Wool dye is fragile and wool felts under moisture + friction. One test now saves a ruined garment later.

homeSolutions.agent: Lift solids — never rub or scrape hard
homeSolutions.instruction: If chocolate is still soft or melty, press a bag of ice against it for 5 minutes until it firms. Then lift solids off with the edge of a plastic card, held flat. Work away from the center. Do NOT scrape, scratch, or rub — friction on damp wool starts felting immediately. The goal is just removing the bulk deposit, not cleaning the fiber yet.

Mix one teaspoon of Eucalan, Woolite, or Soak with two cups of cold water (under 30°C / 86°F). Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and PRESS onto the stain — never rub, never wring the garment. Let sit 5 minutes, then blot. Repeat 2–3 cycles. The detergent does double duty on wool: emulsifies the cocoa butter AND gently lifts the casein without digesting the wool. Plain Dawn also works if that's what you have; just make sure it's clear and fragrance-free.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use OxiClean, Biz, Zout, or any "bio" detergent on wool — they contain enzymes that digest protein fiber along with protein stains.

homeSolutions.agent: White vinegar rinse — restore wool's pH
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Press onto the area with a white cloth. Two minutes dwell. Wool's natural pH is ~5; any detergent residue leaves it alkaline and keeps the scales open, which slowly damages the fiber over days. Vinegar closes the scales back up. On wool this step is load-bearing — skipping it shortens the garment's life even if the stain looks gone.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% peroxide dab — WHITE wool only, strict 60-second ceiling
homeSolutions.instruction: If a tan shadow remains AND the wool is white or cream AND your step-2 patch test was clean: dab 3% hydrogen peroxide mixed 1:1 with cold water on a cotton swab and press onto the shadow. 60 SECONDS MAXIMUM, then flush with cold water immediately. Peroxide can yellow wool if left longer. Skip this step entirely on colored, dark, or patterned wool — and skip it if the patch test showed any dye transfer.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never exceed 60 seconds peroxide on wool. Never mix with ammonia or bleach.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot dry and lay flat — NEVER hang wet wool
homeSolutions.instruction: Press the treated area between dry white towels — stack, press, don't wring. Lay the garment flat on a fresh dry towel, reshape while damp (wool holds the shape it dries in). Do NOT hang wet wool — gravity stretches the fiber. Do NOT use any heat source (dryer, iron, hair dryer). Let it air-dry slowly at room temperature, out of direct sunlight.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a delicates specialist — don't double-dose at home
homeSolutions.instruction: If a shadow persists, or the wool is colored, or the chocolate was set by prior heat exposure, take it to a dry cleaner who specializes in delicates. Do NOT run another round of home chemistry — repeated treatment is the #1 cause of wool felting damage. Tell them: "Chocolate on wool (fat + milk protein + tannin), treated once at home with wool-safe detergent + vinegar + [peroxide on white]. No hot water, no enzymes, no bleach used. Residual [tan/brown] shadow remains." Pros use solvent-based spotting (PERC or hydrocarbon) that's safer on wool than wet chemistry.

materialWarnings: NEVER Protein enzymes—they digest wool's keratin fiber structure. Forbidden entirely.
NEVER hot water above 30°C—wool cuticles swell and lock permanently (felting begins).
NEVER alkaline cleaners (pH > 7)—alkalinity causes cuticles to stand up and lock together.
NEVER chlorine bleach—breaks disulfide bonds in keratin, destroying fiber.
NEVER agitate, rub, or wring—mechanical friction causes irreversible felting.
NEVER apply H₂O₂ to colored wool—bleaching risk.
escalation: Professional dry cleaners can apply solvent-based POG (Petroleum Oil and Grease) spotting to wool chocolate stains at room temperature, avoiding any felting risk. For stubborn tannin discoloration, professionals may use mild tannin reducers formulated specifically for wool or dry-solvent extraction. This is recommended for fine wool, cashmere, or expensive garments.

household.name: Wool-Safe Detergent (pH-neutral, enzyme-free)
household.note: Enzyme-free, pH-neutral wool wash. Household substitute for professional NSD. Lanolin-based; neutral pH; designed to protect wool cuticles. Use at cold temperature.

household.name: Dreft
household.note: Gentle, neutral-pH detergent. Safe for wool fiber. No enzymes.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: Mild acid; mobilizes tannins and prevents felting by smoothing cuticles. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: H₂O₂ 3%
household.note: For yellowing on white wool only. Test first on hidden area. Do NOT use on colored wool.

professional.name: POG (Petroleum Oil and Grease)
professional.note: Dissolves cocoa butter safely without water or heat risk. Professional-only.

professional.name: Wool-Safe Tannin Reducer
professional.note: For stubborn chocolate tannin residue. Formulated for delicate fiber protection.

customerExplanation: Wool fibers have delicate scales that lock together (felting) under heat, alkalinity, or friction—permanent damage. Chocolate has three components: cocoa butter (we dissolved with neutral soap), milk protein (we never enzyme-treated because wool IS protein), and tannin (we mobilized with mild acid). Throughout, we kept water cold, used only gentle blotting, never rubbing, and avoided all heat and alkalinity.
commonMistakes: Using warm water or even 35°C—triggers felting immediately.
Using alkaline cleaners designed for cotton—alkalinity locks wool cuticles together.
Agitating or rubbing—mechanical friction causes irreversible fiber interlocking.
Using Protein—this digests wool's keratin protein, destroying the garment.
Using heat to dry—permanent felting damage.
Hanging to dry—wet wool stretches under gravity and may not recover shape.
scienceNote: Wool's cuticle structure is seven overlapping protein scales per micrometer. Under neutral pH and cool water, these scales lie flat and slide smoothly. When pH rises above 7 (alkalinity) or temperature exceeds 30°C, the scales swell and interlock—cuticle edge catches on cuticle edge and won't release. This is felting: irreversible structural damage. Weak acid (pH 4-5) actually helps prevent felting by smoothing the cuticle back down. Protein enzymes are absolutely forbidden because they cleave the peptide bonds that hold wool's triple-helix structure—identical mechanism to what makes them effective on blood protein, which is why they destroy wool fiber.
callPro: Fine merino wool, cashmere, or expensive wool garments with chocolate stains should see a professional. Wool's felting risk means home treatment requires perfect temperature control, pH management, and mechanical restraint. Professionals have constant-temperature spotting cabinets, pH-controlled solutions, and solvent extraction equipment to guarantee success.
solventNote: Wool tolerates solvent-based treatment better than water-based because organic solvents bypass the pH and temperature sensitivities that trigger felting. Professionals rely on solvent-based POG spotting for wool chocolate stains—risk-free compared to home water chemistry.
aftercare: If chocolate is still moist, blot gently with soft cloth—never rub (friction causes felting). For wet chocolate, apply ice pack wrapped in cloth to stain area for 5-10 minutes to firm it up, then gently lift loosened pieces. DO NOT scrape wool—the cuticle is delicate and metal tools damage fiber surface. This step prevents chocolate from wicking deeper into fiber. Lay wool garment flat on clean towel. Do NOT hang (gravity stretches wet wool and can distort shape). Do NOT use heat. Wool dries slowly but will not retain odor if dried in open air, away from sunlight and heat. Once dry, inspect. If any chocolate tannin remains (brownish shadow), do NOT repeat treatment—call professional dry cleaner who can safely use solvent-based spotting.
